title: Find Pairs with Given Sum in Doubly Linked List

content.problemStatement:
You are given a **sorted doubly linked list** of **distinct positive integers** and an integer **target**.
Your task is to **find all pairs** `(a, b)` such that:

a+b==targeta + b == targeta+b==target  and  a<ba < ba<b

Return or print all such pairs in ascending order of `a`.

If no pairs exist, print an empty list `[]`.

Function DeclarationFunction NamefindPairsfindPairsfindPairs – This function finds all pairs of values in a **sorted doubly linked list** such that:


The sum of the pair equals the given target
The pair satisfies a+b=targeta + b = \text{target}a+b=target and a<ba < ba<b
Parameters
headheadhead : A pointer to the head (first node) of the doubly linked list.
tailtailtail : A pointer to the tail (last node) of the doubly linked list.
targettargettarget : The integer sum for which valid pairs must be found.
Return Value
This function does not return a value.
It prints all valid pairs in the format:

`[a, b] [c, d] ...` in increasing order of `a`.
If no valid pairs exist, it prints `[]`.
Constraints
1≤N≤1051 \leq N \leq 10^51≤N≤105
1≤Node.data≤1051 \leq \text{Node.data} \leq 10^51≤Node.data≤105
1≤target≤1051 \leq \text{target} \leq 10^51≤target≤105
The doubly linked list is sorted in **strictly increasing order**.
All node values are **distinct**.

Input Format

The first line contains an integer NNN — the number of nodes in the doubly linked list.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers representing the node values in sorted order.
The third line contains an integer targettargettarget — the required sum.


Output Format

Print all valid pairs whose sum equals `target`, in the format:

`[a, b] [c, d] ...`
If no such pairs exist, print `[]`.
constraints: The list values are distinct and sorted in strictly increasing order.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases.
For each test case:
- The first line contains an integer N, the number of nodes in the doubly linked list.
- The second line contains N space‑separated integers in strictly increasing order, the node values.
- The third line contains an integer target, the required sum.
All values are positive integers.
content.outputFormat:
For each test case, print all pairs (a, b) such that a + b = target and a < b.
Pairs must be printed in increasing order of a, each in the format "[a, b]" separated by a single space.
If no such pair exists, print "[]".
Print a newline after the output of each test case.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Pairs (1,6) and (2,5) sum to 7 and are printed in increasing order of the first element.
Only the pair (1,5) satisfies the condition.
No two distinct numbers can sum to 3, so an empty list is printed.

hidden.label: edge case – target smaller than any possible sum

solution.approach: Because the list is sorted, we can use the classic two‑pointer technique directly on the doubly linked list. Keep a pointer `left` at the head and a pointer `right` at the tail. At each step compute `sum = left->data + right->data`. If `sum` equals the target, output the pair and move both pointers inward. If `sum` is smaller than the target, advance `left` to increase the sum. If `sum` is larger, move `right` backward to decrease the sum. The process stops when `left` meets or crosses `right`. This runs in O(N) time and O(1) extra space per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of nodes in the list.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) auxiliary space (the list itself uses O(N) memory).
